mnemonics.title: REPL Cycle Steps
mnemonics.content: Remember REPL as 'Reading Enables Programming Learning' - Read input, Evaluate command, Print output, Loop back for more

mnemonics.title: Shell Initialization Order
mnemonics.content: For login shell: Profile Before Login: /etc/profile → ~/.bash_profile → ~/.bash_login → ~/.profile. For non-login: Read RC file (~/.bashrc)

mnemonics.title: Signal Shortcuts
mnemonics.content: Ctrl+C = Cancel (SIGINT), Ctrl+Z = Zero (suspend/SIGTSTP), Ctrl+D = Done (logout)

mnemonics.title: Redirection Rules
mnemonics.content: Greater Than sends out (> output, 2> error), Less Than brings in (< input). Number placement: 2>&1 means error to output stream.

acronyms.usage: The interactive cycle of shell: reads command, evaluates/parses it, prints result, loops for next input
Text-based interface provided by shell for user to interact with OS
Environment variable containing directories to search for executable commands
Unique identifier assigned to each process running in the system
Interrupt signal sent by Ctrl+C to terminate running process
Default input stream, typically keyboard unless redirected
Default output stream, typically screen unless redirected
Default error output stream, typically screen unless redirected

visualTips.visualization: Draw a circular diagram with 4 steps: Input → Parse → Execute → Output, with arrow returning to Input. Label each with examples.
Think of file descriptors as a 3-story building: Floor 0 (stdin) at bottom, Floor 1 (stdout) middle, Floor 2 (stderr) top. Arrows show data flow between floors.
Root is the shell (parent). Child processes branch below. Background jobs shown with horizontal lines, suspended jobs with pause symbol.
Imagine nested boxes: Inner box = shell variables (local), Outer box = environment (exported, visible to children).
Group metacharacters by function: Wildcards (*?) in one group, Redirection (<>) in another, Sequencing (;|&) in third.

analogies.analogy: The shell is like a translator in a court - user speaks in English (commands), translator converts to kernel's language (system calls), delivers message, returns response.
Read = Waiter takes order, Eval = Kitchen prepares (parses and cooks/executes), Print = Waiter serves food (output), Loop = Customer places next order.
Each command in a pipeline is like a worker in an assembly line. Output of one becomes input of next, passing work along until final product emerges.

analogies.anal analogy: Environment variables are like DNA passed from parent cell (shell) to children (child processes) - they carry characteristics/settings to offspring.

analogies.analogy: Foreground job is like a single person working at a desk. Background job is like someone working in another room - accessible but not blocking the main desk.
